questions.question: Distinguish between population and sample. Also define parameter and statistic with examples.
keyPoints: Population is entire set; sample is subset studied
Parameter is population characteristic (unknown, fixed)
Statistic is sample characteristic (known, varies)
Examples: μ is parameter, x̄ is statistic; σ² is parameter, s² is statistic

questions.question: What is statistical inference? Explain its two main branches with examples.
keyPoints: Statistical inference draws conclusions about population from sample data
Estimation: point estimate (x̄ for μ) and interval estimate (confidence intervals)
Hypothesis testing: testing claims about population parameters (H₀ and H₁)
Both use probability theory to quantify uncertainty

questions.question: Define random sampling. Why is it important for valid statistical inference?
keyPoints: Random sampling: each population element has equal chance of selection
Ensures sample is representative of population
Reduces selection bias
Without randomness, inferences may be invalid or misleading
Foundation for calculating probabilities of samples

questions.question: Explain the Central Limit Theorem and its importance in statistical inference.
keyPoints: CLT: For large n, sampling distribution of mean is approximately normal
Works regardless of shape of original population
Enables use of normal-based methods even for non-normal populations
Foundation for confidence intervals and hypothesis testing
Typical threshold: n ≥ 30

questions.question: What is the difference between point estimation and interval estimation?
keyPoints: Point estimation provides single value as estimate of parameter
Interval estimation provides range of values within which parameter likely falls
Interval estimation includes confidence level (e.g., 95% CI)
Point estimate more precise but may miss; interval more reliable but less precise
Example: x̄ = 50 is point estimate; (45, 55) is interval estimate

questions.question: Define standard error. How does sample size affect standard error?
keyPoints: Standard error is standard deviation of sampling distribution
Measures variability of statistic across samples
SE( x̄ ) = σ/√n for sample mean
As sample size n increases, standard error decreases
Larger samples produce more precise estimates (less variability)

questions.question: What is the Law of Large Numbers? How does it relate to statistical inference?
keyPoints: LLN: As sample size increases, sample statistic converges to population parameter
Large samples give more reliable estimates of unknown parameters
Complements CLT (LLN about convergence, CLT about distribution shape)
Foundation for why larger samples are better for inference
Ensures consistency of estimators

questions.question: Define null hypothesis and alternative hypothesis in hypothesis testing. Explain Type I and Type II errors with examples.
keyPoints: Null hypothesis (H₀): statement of no effect/status quo being tested
Alternative hypothesis (H₁): what researcher wants to prove
Type I error: rejecting true H₀ (false positive) - probability = α
Type II error: failing to reject false H₀ (false negative) - probability = β
Example: Testing new drug - Type I = say drug works when it doesn't; Type II = miss actual drug effectiveness
Trade-off between errors: reducing one increases the other

questions.question: Explain the concept of confidence interval. How is it constructed? What factors affect the width of a confidence interval?
keyPoints: CI provides range of plausible values for unknown parameter
Formula: estimate ± (critical value × standard error)
For mean: x̄ ± zα/2 × (σ/√n)
Factors affecting width: confidence level (higher = wider), sample size (larger = narrower), variability (more = wider)
95% CI means if we repeat sampling, 95% of intervals contain true parameter
Trade-off: more confidence = wider interval, larger sample = narrower interval

questions.question: What are the essential requirements for valid statistical inference? Discuss the assumptions underlying common inference methods.
keyPoints: Random sampling: ensures representativeness
Independence of observations: important for probability calculations
Sample size: large enough for CLT or appropriate for small-sample methods
Known or estimated population standard deviation affects method choice
For normal-based inference: normality assumption or large sample (CLT)
Additional assumptions vary: equal variances, categorical vs numerical data, etc.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of sampling distribution. How is it used in statistical inference? Give the formula for standard error of sample mean.
keyPoints: Sampling distribution: probability distribution of a statistic computed from all possible samples
Shows how statistic varies from sample to sample
Enables calculation of probabilities about sample results
For sample mean: if population σ known, sampling distribution is N(μ, σ²/n)
Standard error SE( x̄ ) = σ/√n measures spread of sampling distribution
Used to construct confidence intervals and calculate p-values in hypothesis testing
